answer:
Dunoyer explicitly defines *industrie* (translated as "industry") as far broader than manufacturing or mechanical production, encompassing *all useful labors*—productive activity applied with skill and intelligence to achieve valuable ends, whether upon things (e.g., agriculture, fabrication, commerce) or upon men themselves (e.g., moral, intellectual, scientific, artistic, or political endeavors).[^1] A prime example appears early in his discussion of liberty in a "purely industrious" society, where he clarifies that his subject is not mere mechanical arts but "the liberty possible in a social state where each would be occupied with adding to the value of things *or of men*, and would contribute... to increasing the mass of ideas, of good sentiments, of virtues, [and] utilities of every kind."[^2] Here, *industrie* plainly extends to enhancing human faculties and moral qualities, not just material output—e.g., the work of teachers, judges, moralists, or statesmen who produce "healthy, vigorous, skillful, instructed, virtuous" individuals as accumulated social capital.[^3]

If "industry" were the *only* English equivalent available—forcing a narrow connotation of factories, fabrication, or mechanical toil—Dunoyer's core argument for industrial society as the apex of liberty would lose its philosophical sweep and universality. He portrays it not as a grubby epoch of smokestacks and "mechanical arts" (which critics stupidly caricatured), but as humanity's natural telos: the fullest exercise of faculties in *all* peaceful, value-creating action.[^4] Restricting the term would imply his ideal excludes vital "higher" pursuits (e.g., education, justice, governance as productive "industries"), severing the vital link between broad human *effort/application* (*industrie*'s etymological root in active, skillful construction) and progress toward moral/intellectual freedom.[^5] His vision of society as a harmonious "ensemble of professions," from plowman to magistrate—all mutually dependent producers of utility—would collapse into vulgar materialism, undermining his proof that true liberty emerges precisely when *every* class contributes to species-wide wealth/power.[^6]

[^1]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [paragraph](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-cd2259).

[^2]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [paragraph](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-f7bdb7).

[^3]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, [paragraph](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/13-iii-des-divers-ordres-de-travaux-et-de-fonctions-q/translation/#p-a1cb96).

[^4]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [paragraphs](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-5516e9).

[^5]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [paragraph](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-cd2259).

[^6]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [paragraph](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-f7bdb7).
